quizzes.title: W7 Quiz 1
quizzes.description: Exodus opening links to Genesis, Israel’s multiplication, Moses’ rise, and Yahweh’s self-revelation

questions.question: What does the Hebrew title 'Shemot' mean in relation to Exodus?

options.text: Kings
Names
Covenants
Wonders

questions.explanation: The lecture notes that Exodus begins with 'These are the names' (Eleh Shemot), so the Hebrew title means 'Names.'

questions.question: How does Exodus 1 intentionally echo Genesis according to the lecture?

options.text: By repeating exile language only
By using creation language like fruitful, multiply, and fill
By retelling the flood story
By focusing on Judah’s kings

questions.explanation: Exodus 1:7 is said to echo Genesis with creation-like language describing Israel’s growth.

questions.question: How does the narrative handle the years between Jacob’s family entering Egypt and Moses’ story?

options.text: It gives a detailed year-by-year account
It skips the period entirely and starts with Sinai
It compresses roughly 300–400 years into a few verses
It turns the whole section into poetry

questions.explanation: The lecture emphasizes that Exodus compresses centuries in order to move quickly into the oppression and deliverance narrative.

questions.question: What contrast is drawn between Pharaoh and the Hebrew midwives?

options.text: Pharaoh fears God, while the midwives fear Egypt
Pharaoh is generous, while the midwives are violent
Pharaoh is fearful and wicked, while the midwives fear God and act courageously
Pharaoh and the midwives both resist God

questions.explanation: Pharaoh is portrayed as insecure and oppressive, while the midwives fear God and are blessed.

questions.question: Which repeated verbs frame God’s covenant response near the end of Exodus 2 and into Exodus 3?

options.text: Spoke, wrote, judged
Heard, saw, and knew
Called, sent, and struck
Blessed, fed, and guarded

questions.explanation: The summary highlights God's 'heard / saw / knew' language as a framing device for covenant action.

questions.question: What is the main significance of God's name revelation 'I AM WHO I AM' in the lecture?

options.text: It shows that God is distant from Israel
It ties Yahweh to active, faithful presence with his people
It identifies God only as creator and not redeemer
It cancels the patriarchal promises

questions.explanation: The lecture connects 'I AM' to the verb 'to be' and to God's active presence with Moses and Israel in deliverance.

quizzes.title: W7 Quiz 2
quizzes.description: Exodus as reversal of exile, display of divine sovereignty, and pattern for later salvation

questions.question: What large biblical pattern does the lecture say the Exodus reverses or answers?

options.text: Wisdom and folly
Exile and return
Temple and priesthood
Kingship and monarchy

questions.explanation: The lecture frames the Exodus as a reversal of the Bible’s exile motif, moving from bondage toward the promised land.

questions.question: According to the lecture, over which realms does Exodus especially display God's sovereignty?

options.text: Only over Israel’s worship practices
Only over heavenly beings
Nature, rulers, and death
Trade, cities, and architecture

questions.explanation: The plagues and sea crossing are presented as displays of God’s rule over creation, political powers, and mortality.

questions.question: What is the stated purpose of the plagues in Exodus 9:16 as emphasized in the summary?

options.text: To enrich Israel immediately
To train Egypt in farming
To proclaim God's name in all the earth
To establish Moses as king

questions.explanation: Exodus 9:16 is highlighted as a programmatic verse showing that God's acts proclaim his name throughout the earth.

questions.question: How does the lecture connect Rahab to the Exodus story?

options.text: Rahab ignored the reports about Egypt
Rahab’s faith arose after hearing what God did in Egypt
Rahab is presented as one of the Hebrew midwives
Rahab is used only as an example of disobedience

questions.explanation: The summary states that fear of God's acts in Egypt spread to Canaan and that Rahab’s faith was grounded in hearing those reports.

questions.question: What caution does the lecture give about typology?

options.text: Typology should be avoided completely
Only the New Testament may use types
Who is doing the typing matters
Every biblical event must symbolize baptism

questions.explanation: The lecture stresses that typology must be handled carefully and asks who within Scripture is making the typological connection.

questions.question: What structural conclusion does the lecture draw from Israel before and after the Sinai year?

options.text: The law instantly transforms Israel’s heart
Israel after the law is basically the same as before the law
Sinai ends the wilderness problems permanently
The law removes the need for covenant

questions.explanation: Using Sailhamer’s structural observation, the lecture argues that Israel after Sinai is essentially the same as before, showing the law did not transform the people’s disposition.
